methods.method_name: Base LGM Simulation Setup and Spin-up (CTRL)
methods.description: Process for setting up and spinning up the base Last Glacial Maximum (LGM) climate simulation using the HadCM3 model following the PMIP4 protocol with the GLAC-1D ice sheet reconstruction. This simulation serves as the initial condition for subsequent experiments and as a reference climate state (CTRL).

steps.description: The new HadCM3 LGM simulation was initialized from a chain of existing multi-millennial HadCM3 PMIP3 LGM simulations, which were started from multi-millennial continuations of earlier HadCM3 LGM simulations, giving a pre-PMIP4 LGM spin-up of several thousand years.
steps.searchable_summary: Initialize climate model simulation from previous simulation states
inputs: Existing HadCM3 PMIP3 LGM simulation states

steps.description: The new PMIP4 GLAC-1D set-up was integrated for 3,500 years, and the end of this final spin-up phase provides the initial condition for all simulations presented here.
steps.searchable_summary: Run climate model simulation for a specified spin-up period
outputs: Spun-up HadCM3 LGM simulation state (initial condition)

steps.description: We continued the LGM simulation for a further 4,000 years in parallel with our other experiments to provide a reference climate state (CTRL) for comparison to the other simulations.
steps.searchable_summary: Continue base LGM simulation as a reference run
inputs: Spun-up HadCM3 LGM simulation state (initial condition)
outputs: CTRL simulation output (climate state over 4000 years)

steps.description: The starting year of CTRL is defined as year 0.

methods.description: Method for setting up and running six equilibrium-type climate simulations using HadCM3, starting from the base LGM state, forced with constant meltwater discharge scenarios derived from a transient deglacial history. This approach investigates the sensitivity of the glacial ocean and surface climate to early deglacial freshwater inputs.

steps.description: A transient meltwater history was derived from GLAC-1D's representation of the deglaciation (Section S2 in Supporting Information S1).
steps.searchable_summary: Derive transient meltwater history from ice sheet reconstruction data

steps.description: We examined the triggering of abrupt climate changes using a simpler approach; by selecting six different, fixed-forcing scenarios (our "snapshots"), that allow us to investigate the sensitivity of the glacial ocean and surface climate to early deglacial freshwater inputs (Figure 1a). The snapshots were identified for their ability to collectively capture a broad range of possible situations that may have led to changes in ocean circulation and surface climate. The six scenarios correspond to different modes of discharge and are named after the period they were extracted from (see Figure 1a); see Figure 1c for the spatial distribution of the fluxes.
steps.searchable_summary: Select specific time slices from transient meltwater history as fixed forcing scenarios
outputs: Six fixed meltwater forcing scenarios (spatial distribution, rate, time period name)

steps.description: These six snapshots of the deglacial meltwater history were used as forcing for six new equilibrium-type (i.e., constant-forcing) simulations, started from year 0.
steps.searchable_summary: Configure climate model simulations with constant meltwater forcing
Initialize new simulations from a common initial state
inputs: Initial condition state (from Base LGM Simulation Setup, Step 2)
outputs: Initialized HadCM3 simulations ready to run

steps.description: The meltwater was discharged into the surface layer of the ocean and kept constant throughout the runs.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply constant meltwater forcing to ocean surface layer during simulation integration
outputs: Running HadCM3 simulations with meltwater forcing

methods.description: Method used to counteract global freshening caused by the long-term continuous freshwater forcing in the meltwater simulations by imposing a constant global mean salinity target, following the VFLUX method.

steps.description: To avoid long-term drifts in mean ocean salinity caused by the long freshwater forcing, we impose a constant global mean salinity target (following the VFLUX method of Dentith et al. [2019]) commensurate with the starting condition for each "snapshot" (Table 1).
steps.searchable_summary: Set global mean salinity target for each simulation based on starting conditions
inputs: Starting condition salinity for each snapshot simulation
outputs: Global mean salinity target value for each simulation

steps.description: The salinity target conserves water in relation to terrestrial ice volume (applied as relative to the present day) and thus, in the context of these simulations, counteracts global freshening by removing the excess water as a very small proportion of freshwater from every ocean grid cell at every ocean model timestep (1 hr).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply salinity restoration by removing excess freshwater globally at each model timestep

methods.description: Procedure for determining the duration of the climate model simulations based on standard practice, observed stability, and analysis requirements.

steps.description: Most simulations were run for 10,000 years; long enough to characterize their climate behaviors.
steps.searchable_summary: Run climate model simulations for a standard duration of 10,000 years

steps.description: However, like CTRL, two simulations (21.5k and 20.7k), were terminated after 4,000 years. At this point, little was changing in those simulations, and since no further time series were required for the analysis, we opted to conserve the computing resource.
steps.searchable_summary: Terminate specific simulations early based on stability and analysis needs
inputs: Simulation output (e.g., time series of key variables)

methods.description: Procedure to identify and resolve numerical instability observed in some simulations, specifically a stream function runaway off the coast of the Philippines.

steps.description: Some simulations experienced numerical instability after a few thousand years observed through a stream function runaway off the coast of the Philippines.
steps.searchable_summary: Monitor simulation output for signs of numerical instability, such as stream function runaway
outputs: Identification of instability location and time

steps.description: This quirk was resolved by smoothing the bathymetry in the region of the instability
steps.searchable_summary: Modify model bathymetry data by smoothing in a specific region

steps.description: and restarting the run a few years before the instability arose.
steps.searchable_summary: Restart simulation from a previous stable state after model configuration modification
